text: The laparoscopic view reveals purulent peritonitis and a perforation in the sigmoid colon, with visible pus and inflamed tissue surrounding the perforation site.
The surgeon begins the medial to lateral mobilization of the descending colon, carefully separating it from the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon sections the inferior mesenteric vein and detaches the mesocolon of the descending colon from the deep plane, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
Following the complete mobilization of the ascending colon, the surgeon identifies the left ureter, ensuring it is clearly visualized and preserved during the procedure.
After mobilizing the mesosigmoid, the surgeon ensures that the artery is not sectioned at its origin, preserving the vascularization of the area.
The surgeon uses a stapler to section the colon at the promontory level, visibly dividing the tissue.
The surgeon sections the mesocolon adjacent to the colon, continuing the dissection process in the colorectal region.
The surgeon performs a mechanical circular anastomosis, connecting the previously sectioned segments of the colon using a circular stapling device.
The surgeon detaches a reinforcement point, likely related to the previously completed anastomosis or mobilization steps.
